content.plain_english: This week your child is naming and describing 2D shapes (flat shapes like squares and triangles) and 3D shapes (solid shapes like cubes and cylinders). They are learning to count sides, edges, vertices and faces, and to spot lines of symmetry.
content.in_school: Children handle real 3D shapes — boxes, balls, tubes — counting faces, edges and vertices by touch. They press faces against paper to print 2D outlines, talk about which 2D shape lives on each face, and fold paper to find lines of symmetry.
content.home_activity: On the next supermarket trip, give your child a one-minute scavenger hunt: find one cuboid (a cereal box), one cylinder (a tin), one sphere (an orange), one cube (a stock cube). At home, ask them to count the faces of each. End by asking which shape rolls and which does not, and why.
dinner_table_questions: How many faces does a cereal box have? How many edges?
Can you find something on the table that has a line of symmetry?
What is the difference between a cube and a cuboid?

key_vocabulary.definition: A solid shape with length, width and depth. A football is 3D, while a square drawn on paper is 2D (flat).
A flat side of a 3D shape. A cube has 6 faces.
Where two faces of a 3D shape meet. A cube has 12 edges.
A corner of a shape, where edges meet. A cube has 8 vertices. The plural is vertices.
A line that splits a shape into two halves that are mirror images of each other.

example_questions: How many sides does a pentagon have?
Name a 3D shape that has only curved surfaces and no edges.
Draw a line of symmetry on a square. How many lines of symmetry does it have?
content.times_table_tip: A square has 4 sides, a hexagon has 6, an octagon has 8. Counting up in 4s and 8s while looking at shapes helps the times tables stick.
